anchor: Anthropic's Claude Code — 512,000 lines of source, the tool millions of developers use daily — leaked through a known Bun bug (#28001, open 20 days) that serves source maps in production by default. Community analysis (2,002 HN points, one of the highest threads of 2026) revealed unreleased capabilities: KAIROS, a persistent background agent mode; 'dream mode' for continuous reasoning across sessions; anti-distillation fake tools that poison competitor training data; and frustration-detection regexes that flag when users say 'wtf' or 'broken.' Same 48 hours: Axios (83M weekly downloads) was compromised via stolen maintainer credentials with a cross-platform RAT, and LiteLLM's supply chain attack leaked 4TB of Mercor ($10B valuation) data after poisoned PyPI versions exfiltrated SSH keys and cloud credentials across an estimated 36% of cloud environments. Three different npm failure modes — accidental build artifact exposure, stolen credentials, and CI/CD pipeline poisoning — in one weekend.
angle: The npm ecosystem just demonstrated three independent failure modes in 48 hours, and the common thread is that the default trust model assumes good actors at every step. Bun's defaults exposed source code. npm's publish flow accepted stolen credentials. A CI/CD pipeline was the vector for the LiteLLM attack. This isn't about any single incident — the entire dependency pipeline is now a primary attack surface, and every team running agentic coding is in the blast radius.
connection: paddo.dev's 'npm Had a Very Bad Day' analysis frames the Claude Code leak and Axios attack as two sides of the same structural problem: npm is simultaneously too trusting (stolen credentials publish arbitrary code) and too transparent (build artifacts ship to anyone who asks). Neither incident required sophisticated exploits — both exploited default behavior.
reaction: I run Claude Code sessions that touch every file in my codebase. When I saw the leak, my first thought wasn't about KAIROS or dream mode — it was about my own .npmignore files. I build with Bun. I publish packages. The bug that exposed Anthropic is the same default I've been running with. I checked my build output within an hour of reading the HN thread. The anti-distillation system is fascinating from an agent builder's perspective — I've been thinking about poisoning defenses for my own pipeline — but the real story is that a 20-day-old known bug in the build tool caused the biggest source leak of the year.
open_questions: How many other npm packages built with Bun are currently shipping source maps without knowing it?
Did anyone actually exploit the KAIROS or dream mode code, or is this just embarrassing rather than dangerous?
The LiteLLM attack hit 36% of cloud environments — how many companies haven't discovered they were affected yet?
do_not_include: Claude Code v2.1.89 release notes (incremental update)
mindpattern_context: I run agents on a daily cron that pull from npm dependencies. The LiteLLM attack vector — CI/CD pipeline compromise → malicious PyPI publish — is structurally identical to what could hit any automated pipeline that pins to latest.

anchor: More than 100 Baidu Apollo Go robotaxis froze mid-traffic in Wuhan — on highways, in intersections, in fast-moving lanes — trapping passengers inside for up to two hours. Police received a surge of emergency calls from people afraid to open their doors. Bloomberg confirmed at least one crash. Baidu blamed a 'system safety check.' Same week, ARC-AGI-3 launched a new interactive puzzle format — no instructions, no rules, just exploration — and every frontier AI model collapsed from high ARC-AGI-2 scores to below 1%. Best model score: 0.26%. Humans: 100%. The $2M prize now specifically rewards the exploratory reasoning that a robotaxi needs when something unexpected happens on a highway at 60 mph.
angle: The confidence gap between AI benchmarks and physical reality just went from abstract to visceral. We've been deploying AI systems at scales that assume verified capability, and this week proved we're in the 'confidently wrong' phase. The ARC-AGI-3 collapse isn't an academic curiosity — it's the empirical evidence that high benchmark scores reflect format optimization, not the adaptive reasoning you need when 100 vehicles freeze in traffic simultaneously.
connection: ARC-AGI-3's interactive format proved every frontier model's benchmark scores were format-dependent — move from static puzzles to interactive exploration and performance collapses 99%+. The $2M prize now requires exactly the capability that was missing in Wuhan: explore, learn rules from scratch, and adapt when the situation changes.
reaction: I review every diff my agents generate before it touches my codebase. I've caught enough wrong outputs to know that confident ≠ correct. But I'm dealing with text files, not highways. The Baidu failure is what 'AI hallucinates' looks like when the hallucination is a two-ton vehicle stopping in the fast lane. I keep thinking about Anthropic's own internal study that showed junior devs using AI scored 17% lower on mastery quizzes — the pattern is the same at every scale. We're building confidence faster than we're building verification.
open_questions: Was the Baidu 'safety check' actually a fail-safe that worked as intended, or a euphemism for a fleet-wide crash?
How many other autonomous deployments have had similar incidents that weren't in a dense urban area with Bloomberg reporters?
Does the ARC-AGI-3 collapse actually predict real-world agent failure, or is it measuring a different kind of reasoning?
do_not_include: Greptile 'slop is not the future' debate (related but dilutes the physical stakes)

anchor: Salesforce just shipped the most aggressive enterprise AI overhaul since the $27.7B Slack acquisition — 30+ new AI features including a desktop monitoring agent that watches your screen activity and drafts follow-ups based on what it sees. Same week, Perplexity AI was hit with a class-action lawsuit: Bloomberg reports trackers were downloaded to user devices upon login, sharing conversations with Meta and Google even in Perplexity's Incognito mode. You signed up for a search engine. You got a surveillance pipeline. You got a Slack upgrade. You got a screen recorder. The consent gap between what AI tools promise and what they collect is now a federal lawsuit.
angle: Enterprise AI adoption is being sold as productivity. The actual product is behavioral data. Salesforce's desktop monitoring and Perplexity's incognito-mode sharing aren't bugs — they're the business model for tools that can't yet prove ROI through output quality alone. When the AI can't justify its cost through results, it justifies its existence through data collection. The consent gap is the story.
connection: Bloomberg reports Perplexity AI was sued in federal court after trackers allegedly gave Meta and Google full access to user conversations — including in Incognito mode. Amazon simultaneously secured a court order blocking Perplexity's Comet browser from accessing its services.
reaction: I use Slack every day. When I read the desktop monitoring detail, I thought about how many Claude Code sessions I've run with Slack open in the background. Screen activity drafting follow-ups means the agent isn't just in your chat window — it's watching everything else you're doing. I built my own pipeline specifically so my research workflow doesn't depend on tools I can't audit. The Perplexity lawsuit is the quiet part out loud: if you're not paying enough to cover the compute, you're paying with your data. Even if you turned on Incognito.
open_questions: Is the Salesforce desktop monitoring opt-in or opt-out by default?
How many enterprise IT teams will actually disable the monitoring feature vs. quietly leaving it on?
Is the Perplexity tracker allegation technically accurate, or is this a misreading of standard analytics SDKs?
do_not_include: Claude Code leak privacy details from The Register (overlaps with Topic 1, April 1 publication date adds uncertainty)
Dario Amodei Australia MOU (policy, different angle)
CSA governance report showing 68% can't distinguish AI from human (enterprise survey, too dry)
Claude Code quota crisis (Anthropic operational issue, not surveillance)
